title: A Leak of San Francisco Police Drone Footage Exposes the New Reality of Urban Surveillance
gist: A misconfigured Skydio 'ReadyLink' shareable-video feature exposed roughly six months of live SFPD drone footage, GPS telemetry, and pilot identities to the open internet, discoverable via a routine OSINT tool rather than any hack; the leak reveals SFPD's drone fleet has grown from 6 to 98 units since 2024 with over 1,400 flights, and shows AI-scale video analysis now makes true mass surveillance of bystanders practically feasible.

insights.claim: A misconfigured Skydio 'ReadyLink,' a shareable link feature intended for internal SFPD coordination, was created last December with no authentication requirement and a one-year expiration, exposing live video, thermal imaging, GPS telemetry, and drone pilots' names and emails from five SFPD surveillance drones to the open internet for roughly six months before researchers discovered it.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: A concrete case study of a specific product misconfiguration, no-auth links with long expirations, in a widely deployed public-safety drone platform, a direct cybersecurity and product-design risk for Skydio as a vendor to hundreds of police departments.
insights.quote: Someone with access to the SFPD's instance of Skydio's software, however, appears to have created a link last December to five of its drones' feeds with no authentication requirement and an expiration date of one full year.
insights.locator: Curry and Robert say the drone videos were exposed

insights.claim: The exposed link had already been indexed in AlienVault Open Threat Exchange, a security-research archive of web URLs, meaning the SFPD's live drone feeds were discoverable via a standard open-source intelligence tool rather than through any sophisticated hacking or authentication bypass.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that sensitive government surveillance infrastructure can be exposed through routine OSINT reconnaissance rather than active hacking, a low-bar risk vector relevant to any vendor selling cloud-linked hardware to government or critical-infrastructure customers.
insights.quote: they used the tool GetAllURLs, which pulls all archived web addresses for a given domain from sources including AlienVault Open Threat Exchange
insights.locator: Curry and Robert say they first became curious about Skydio

themes.theme: Scale of SFPD's Drone Surveillance Program

insights.claim: SFPD's drone-as-first-responder fleet has scaled rapidly since its 2024 launch, growing from 6 to 98 drones with more than 1,400 flight launches logged between May 2024 and March 2026.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Quantifies the growth trajectory of police drone programs at the city level, a useful adoption-curve benchmark for Skydio's addressable market and for tracking surveillance-capacity buildout across US police departments more broadly.
insights.quote: SFPD's fleet has expanded from six drones to 98, and officers logged more than 1,400 launches between May 2024 and March 2026
insights.locator: Skydio, based in nearby San Mateo

insights.claim: Automated image analysis of the leaked footage found hundreds of people and vehicles captured across 20 flights, including 34 people visible in a single downtown-intersection frame and dozens of clearly identifiable faces, showing routine police drone missions incidentally sweep up large numbers of uninvolved bystanders, not just investigation targets.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Quantifies incidental bystander surveillance at scale, a concrete data point for any privacy-regulation or product-liability analysis of urban drone programs.
insights.quote: In a single frame, as a drone hovered over a downtown intersection, the software counted 34 people crossing the street or standing on the sidewalks.
insights.locator: WIRED analyzed all 20 color videos with software

themes.theme: Structural Privacy Risk from Mass Drone + AI Surveillance

insights.claim: ACLU policy analyst Jay Stanley frames all law-enforcement surveillance video as a 'toxic asset' that is inherently at risk of breach, and recommends departments minimize what they record in the first place rather than relying on after-the-fact access controls.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: A risk-management principle applicable beyond this incident: any organization retaining large volumes of sensitive surveillance data carries latent breach liability proportional to data volume, arguing for data-minimization as the primary mitigation.
insights.quote: any law enforcement surveillance data a "toxic asset" that's always at risk of a security breach
insights.locator: The fact that so much San Francisco drone footage was potentially exposed online

insights.claim: Stanley argues AI materially raises the stakes of bulk video collection because, unlike human reviewers who lack time to watch every frame, AI systems can scalably analyze the entirety of mass surveillance footage, turning previously impractical blanket surveillance into a practical capability.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Identifies AI video-analysis capability as the mechanism that converts raw camera and drone deployment into true mass-surveillance capacity, relevant for evaluating the compounding privacy risk of pairing drone fleets like Skydio's with computer-vision analytics products.
insights.quote: Maybe no human has the time to pore over every frame of these videos, but an AI can do that, and it's scalable to vast amounts of video
insights.locator: Watching these videos, it was just a reminder of what a powerful technology this is

themes.theme: Legal & Policy Precedent on Drone Footage Disclosure

insights.claim: A California appeals court rejected Chula Vista PD's argument that drone footage could be categorically withheld from public-records requests, and the state supreme court declined to review it, creating statewide precedent that police drone video is not automatically exempt from disclosure.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Establishes binding California legal precedent narrowing police departments' ability to blanket-deny drone footage requests, a regulatory tailwind for transparency advocates and a litigation-risk factor for CA police drone programs going forward.
insights.quote: A California appeals court rejected the city's position that the videos could be categorically withheld, a ruling that became statewide precedent
insights.locator: In fact, police departments have very often treated drone footage as too sensitive

insights.claim: SFPD's official transparency portal publishes post-hoc flight logs but never video, meaning the public has no routine visibility into actual drone footage content; the leak was the first time footage itself, rather than metadata, became publicly visible, exposing a structural transparency gap.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Highlights that current transparency mechanisms for police drone programs are largely metadata-only, meaning meaningful public oversight of actual surveillance content currently depends on incidents like this leak rather than routine disclosure, a policy gap open to reform.
insights.quote: The city maintains a transparency portal that publishes information about police drone flights, including logs, after they occur—without video.
insights.locator: The city maintains a transparency portal

insights.claim: Despite SFPD policy requiring operators to avoid recording areas where people have a reasonable expectation of privacy, the exposed footage showed full missions from takeoff to landing capturing streets, apartment buildings, rooftops, and bystanders unrelated to any police operation, indicating a gap between stated policy and actual operational practice.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Suggests SFPD's drone privacy policy is not reliably enforced in practice, a compliance and governance risk relevant to assessing legal liability exposure for the department and, by extension, reputational risk for Skydio as its technology vendor.
insights.quote: the exposed Skydio feeds reviewed by WIRED showed full missions from takeoff to landing, capturing not only detentions and searches, but also streets, apartment buildings, rooftops, cars, courtyards, and bystanders
insights.locator: SFPD's drone policy says operators must keep cameras trained

insights.claim: In several documented cases, drones were dispatched to minor or ultimately unfounded calls, such as two men playing basketball after a 'suspicious person in a vehicle' report and a person on a rooftop wearing headphones flagged as a 'prowler,' raising the question of whether the scale of aerial surveillance is proportionate to the underlying incidents being investigated.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Provides concrete examples supporting a proportionality critique of drone-as-first-responder programs, useful for anticipating future civil-liberties litigation or local policy pushback that could constrain program scale and growth.
insights.quote: That one felt like an invasion of privacy, just so uncomfortable... Like this person thinks they're by themselves on this roof and has gotten away from everybody, and then there's a police drone watching them.
insights.locator: A drone flight in response to what police records describe as a 'person with a gun' investigation
